result.markdown:
--- title: "Host-Virus Interaction Networks" subtitle: "Species interaction networks from GBIF and GLOBI; potyvirus host range in ecological context" author: "chrissi m." date: last-modified format: html: toc: true code-fold: true execute: echo: true warning: false message: false --- ## Introduction To check whether interactions between species harbouring viral flanking sequences could explain the distribution of homologous loci, I built multilayer networks connecting: 1. **Species–host plant** ecological interactions (from EPPO / GLOBI) 2. **Shared-host** projections (species sharing a common host) 3. **Phylogenetic** relationships (Open Tree of Life) See also the interactive [BioNexus Shiny app](../shiny/bionexus/) which queries GBIF and GLOBI in real time. ## Part 1: Bipartite species–host network ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} species_hosts <- list( "Fallopia convolvulus" = character(0), "Luperina nickerlii" = c("Elytrigia juncea", "Poaceae grasses"), "Luperina testacea" = c("Poaceae grasses", "Dactylis glomerata"), "Trichiura crataegi" = c("Prunus spinosa", "Crataegus", "Salix", "Carpinus"), "Sphenella marginata" = c("Senecio/Jacobaea ragworts") ) edges <- enframe(species_hosts, name = "species", value = "hosts") %>% unnest(hosts, keep_empty = TRUE) host_nodes <- edges$hosts[!is.na(edges$hosts)] bip_graph <- graph_from_data_frame( d = edges %>% filter(!is.na(hosts)), vertices = tibble( name = c(names(species_hosts), unique(host_nodes)), type = c( rep(FALSE, length(species_hosts)), rep(TRUE, length(unique(host_nodes))) ) ), directed = FALSE ) stopifnot(!any(is.na(V(bip_graph)$type))) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggraph(bip_graph, layout = "bipartite") + geom_edge_link() + geom_node_point(aes(color = type), size = 6) + geom_node_text(aes(label = name), repel = TRUE, size = 4) + scale_color_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "steelblue", "TRUE" = "darkorange"), labels = c("species", "host plant")) + theme_void() + ggtitle("Species–Host Bipartite Network") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](05b_networks_files/figure-html/plot-bipartite-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} proj <- bipartite_projection(bip_graph) species_graph <- proj$proj1 ggraph(species_graph, layout = "fr") + geom_edge_link() + geom_node_point(color = "steelblue", size = 8) + geom_node_text(aes(label = name), repel = TRUE, size = 4) + theme_void() + ggtitle("Species-only Projected Network") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](05b_networks_files/figure-html/species-projection-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: # Part 2: Multilayer network with phylogenetic and ecological edges ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} species <- names(species_hosts) eco_edges <- enframe(species_hosts, name = "species", value = "hosts") %>% unnest(hosts, keep_empty = TRUE) hosts <- unique(eco_edges$hosts[!is.na(eco_edges$hosts)]) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Match species names to OTT IDs via the Open Tree of Life API (network). # Wrapped so the chapter still renders offline / if the service is down — # phylo_edges then falls back to empty and the multilayer network simply # omits the phylogenetic layer. phylo_edges <- tryCatch({ taxa <- rotl::tnrs_match_names(species) ott_ids <- taxa$ott_id[!is.na(taxa$ott_id)] tree <- rotl::tol_induced_subtree(ott_ids = ott_ids) tibble( from = tree$tip.label[tree$edge[, 1]], to = tree$tip.label[tree$edge[, 2]] ) %>% filter(!is.na(from), !is.na(to)) %>% mutate(type = "phylogenetic", weight = 1) }, error = function(e) { message("Open Tree of Life query unavailable (", conditionMessage(e), "); omitting the phylogenetic layer.") tibble(from = character(), to = character(), type = character(), weight = numeric()) }) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pair_grid <- expand.grid(sp1 = species, sp2 = species, stringsAsFactors = FALSE) shared_host_edges <- pair_grid %>% filter(sp1 < sp2) %>% mutate( shared = map2_int(sp1, sp2, ~ length(intersect( species_hosts[[.x]], species_hosts[[.y]]))) ) %>% filter(shared > 0) %>% transmute(from = sp1, to = sp2, type = "shared_host", weight = shared) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} eco_edges2 <- eco_edges %>% filter(!is.na(hosts)) %>% transmute(from = species, to = hosts, type = "ecological", weight = 1) all_edges <- bind_rows(eco_edges2, phylo_edges, shared_host_edges) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} traits <- tibble( name = c(species, hosts), trophic = case_when( name %in% species ~ "herbivore", name %in% hosts ~ "plant", TRUE ~ "unknown"), mobility = case_when( name %in% species ~ "flying", name %in% hosts ~ "sessile", TRUE ~ "unknown"), taxon = case_when( name %in% species ~ "Insecta", name %in% hosts ~ "Plantae", TRUE ~ "internal") ) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} nodes <- traits %>% transmute(id = name, label = name, group = trophic, mobility = mobility, taxon = taxon) edges_vis <- all_edges %>% transmute( from, to, value = weight, title = paste(type, "weight =", weight), color = case_when( type == "ecological" ~ "darkorange", type == "phylogenetic" ~ "forestgreen", type == "shared_host" ~ "steelblue")) visNetwork(nodes, edges_vis, height = "900px", width = "100%") %>% visEdges(smooth = FALSE) %>% visOptions(highlightNearest = TRUE, nodesIdSelection = TRUE) %>% visLegend() %>% visPhysics(stabilization = TRUE) %>% visLayout(randomSeed = 123) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## BioNexus Shiny App The BioNexus v3.0 Shiny application provides an interactive interface for querying GBIF and GLOBI interaction data for arbitrary species lists and constructing multilayer networks with multiplex participation coefficients. The app lives at [`shiny/bionexus/app.R`](../shiny/bionexus/app.R). To launch: ```r shiny::runApp(here::here("shiny", "bionexus")) ``` Key features: - GBIF occurrence mapping for user-supplied species - GLOBI biotic interaction retrieval - Multilayer network construction (ecological + phylogenetic + shared-host layers) - Multiplex participation coefficient for identifying bridge species - Interactive visualisation via `visNetwork` ## Summary The network analysis places the species harbouring viral-homologous loci within a connected ecological network. Pollinators and their host plants — including known potyvirus hosts — cluster together, consistent with the hypothesis that viral flanking sequences were transferred horizontally via a shared biological vector during co-infection events.
